output: Thank you, Mr. President. Uh, I very much welcome the approach we describe in the White Paper, namely that we get to the root causes of malnutrition and obesity and the diseases resulting therefrom.

Unfortunately, in European food policy, we are falling back into a very one-sided approach, regardless of whether we are discussing nutrient profiles, food labeling, or even in some parts of the White Paper.
We are trying to solve the problems we have in Europe through product policy.
and I believe that it is a fundamentally wrong political approach to assume that there are good and bad foods.
Many amendment proposals show this. That doesn't exist.
There are only good and bad, or also balanced and unbalanced ways of nutrition.
That should be our share.
The causes of improper nutrition are multifaceted.

To remedy them by trying to guide the consumer through product labeling, or even by proceeding with advertising bans or regulations, or indeed by implementing different VAT rates, will not lead us to our goal.

I believe everything we do here will not pre-empt the labeling regulation debate, but everything we do here should be somewhat under the heading of what we can do to motivate the consumer to think about their eating habits.

A traffic light label that suggests to him, "I am now standing before a good or a bad product," is, in my opinion, rather an instrument of disempowerment than an instrument of education and enlightenment.
That is why I would like to see a more balanced approach here and the realization that the path solely through product policy will not get us any further here. Many thanks.
President, Madam Vice-President, dear colleagues, the budgetary control has no new topics.
We are satisfied with the old ones and would also be satisfied with progress on the old topics.
Five points: firstly, our urgent request for the support of Romania and Bulgaria in building up the rule of law, the justice system, and financial control.
We do not want to be where we are today in two years, and therefore this point is very important to us.
The second point is the statement of assurance for the entire budget, according to the tiered control procedure, involving the national audit institutions.
It would be nice if, in the last half-year that the Commission only has left, we could achieve decisive progress here, or at least could agree on a common path.
As a household auditor, one certainly becomes modest.
The third point would be the simplification.
We must provide more help to the lower administrative levels of the member states in the implementation of EU law.
It shows that they are completely overwhelmed by large parts of EU law, which also explains the high error rates.
The fourth point, foreign aid. Here I would primarily like to address Kosovo and the question of cooperation with the United Nations.
The Committee on Budgetary Control has been to Kosovo, and I would like to announce now that Kosovo will play a certain role in the budget discharge procedure.
I believe that we have remained far below the capabilities of the European Union until now, and that we urgently need to catch up here.
The fifth point would be the improvement of cooperation among member states in combating fraud.
We have homework here, especially for the ladies and gentlemen of the council.
The parliament will vote in November on the legal basis, the regulation for combating fraud, and then we must jointly consider how we proceed if we wanted to achieve better results in this field.
That was, until now at least, our common
Thank you, Mr. President. India and Europe need more and better contacts with each other at all levels. Especially at the level of so-called ordinary people.
Business travelers, tourists, students, and so on. A brief remark on that. It also has significance for the Commission's legislative program, which we discussed earlier.
In worldwide air traffic, we need good safety regulations, ones that achieve something and are not just mere token gestures.
It would therefore be a reasonable, truly necessary measure to finally abolish the senseless liquid regulations and come up with something better.
I voted for the resolution because it is a clear call to the Belarusian government to finally respect human rights.

And secondly, I would have wished for a clearer, more unambiguous report from the Employment Committee.
Trust in the social cohesive force of the European Union can only be achieved if fundamental social rights are defined as European primary law.

We should send a stronger signal to the Council, the Commission, the Member States, and the ECJ, and not be content with merely demanding a balance between fundamental rights and single market freedoms, and freedom of movement.

We have someone with her who, hopefully, doesn't actually spill their heart's blood, but invests a great deal of heart's blood into this room.

In this connection, an important task falls to the ombudsman, especially then and there, when and where he performs it well.

on the other hand, it is about ensuring that wherever shortcomings have occurred in the administration of the European institutions, these are addressed and, where possible, reasonable solutions are sought, which can then reassure and satisfy the citizens.

Dear colleagues, 10 years after the realization of the Monetary Union, Europe finds itself in a severe crisis.
Banks are collapsing, or are being propped up with billions from the state budget. Sales are plummeting, and millions of people fear for their jobs and their future.
But not only the market has failed. The ruling political class also seems unable to learn from its mistakes.

We are of the opinion that even with such interjections, you will not particularly distinguish yourselves. We are of the opinion that grave errors were made in the construction of the Economic and Monetary Union.

Such a mistake is the structural disintegration of monetary and fiscal policy. One cannot create a common currency without simultaneously harmonizing tax and spending policy, at least in its basic elements.
I think the economic imbalances in the Eurozone have significantly worsened.
And what we now need is actually better coordination of economic and, above all, tax policy.
We need effective measures against tax dumping. Furthermore, tax havens must be closed and capital flows must finally be controlled again.
We see the second serious error in the construction of the Stability and Growth Pact.

Whoever in times like these considers a consolidation of budgets unavoidable, apparently lives in another world. Nothing would be more fatal than to react to this economic crisis now also with public austerity programs.
The Stability Pact has quite clearly demonstrated its failure.
It should be replaced by an integrated European strategy for solidarity and sustainable development.
What is needed, in our opinion, is an investment offensive for the renewal of public infrastructure and for the improvement of the situation of the socially disadvantaged in Europe.
A third mistake we see in the construction of the European Central Bank itself, which is not subject to any democratic control and is solely committed to the goal of price stability.

We advocate for the democratic control of the European Central Bank, and furthermore, we demand a correction of the ECB's monetary policy mandate, whereby in the future, growth and employment must be promoted at least on an equal footing with price stability.
The current crisis also offers an opportunity for far-reaching reforms of the European monetary and financial architecture.
This opportunity should not be carelessly squandered.
Thank you very much, Madam President, Mr. Commissioner, Mr. President of the Council, distinguished colleagues. Indeed, the automotive industry is in crisis, and there are many reasons for this.
In particular, the financial crisis has once again exacerbated the crisis in this sector, and in the member states, solutions are currently being worked on.
The European automotive industry is a key industry.
And therefore, for this European key industry, we also need European solutions to overcome the crisis.
Almost all car manufacturers exhibit European structures.

And to enable necessary investments, environmentally friendly cars – this has been mentioned several times today – to implement environmentally friendly and fuel-efficient technologies, car manufacturers need public guarantees or low-interest loans from the European Investment Bank.
But also the supplier industry, which is very important to me, must not be forgotten in this context.
especially in the supplier industry, it is small and medium-sized enterprises where jobs are created and where innovations take place.
But what can the European Union do to protect this key industry?
In the past plenary session, the President of the Council made a proposal to protect key industries.
A good proposal, in my opinion, if one consistently thinks this idea, this proposal through to its conclusion, it means a Volkswagen law for the entire European automotive industry.
And this would certainly be a good instrument.
But this protection instrument is currently being combated by the European Commission.
And so my question to the Commission: Will the European Commission, despite the crisis in the automotive industry, stick to a lawsuit, or will it now change this course in light of the crisis?
Yes, Mr. President, Mr. President of the European Court of Auditors, Vice-President of the European Commission, dear colleagues, indeed, it has now become a tradition to state
may, that in the relationship between expenditure at the European level and administration at the national level, a declaration of reliability cannot be issued yet again.

I can recall conversations with the former President of the Court of Auditors, Professor Friedmann, who once told me that, due to the structures, it is not possible at all for a declaration of reliability to be given.

Therefore, we should already think together about how this instrument can also be imbued with life in such a way that the objective, a declaration of reliability, can also be achieved if it is justified.
And there we must, of course, distinguish a few things from one another.
Firstly, a budget that includes around 95% subsidies is much more susceptible to fraud than a national budget, or a regional or a municipal budget.

A subsidy budget, which is essentially managed by the member states, who in the new financial period have demanded much more autonomy from us, because the accusation of the last funding period was that too much was centrally controlled.
This also means, of course, that the responsibility for the money must be assumed regionally and nationally.
I would just like to briefly address the third topic area below.
We must also learn to distinguish between fraud and waste.
whose things are always often lumped together.
I also get annoyed when things are funded with EU money that are not strictly necessary.
However, that was not fraud; that was waste.

Therefore, those who are responsible for the projects, especially in the area of structural policy—the member states—also bear the responsibility to ensure that the money is not wasted and that only those projects are funded that truly generate added value for the region.
Therefore, I believe we should also certainly consider converting parts of structural policy to interest-free loans,
If member states have to pay back, then they will only fund what they genuinely need. Thank you very much.
It's not just me who finds it difficult to join in with this emerging self-praise of European decision-makers when it comes to overcoming the financial market crisis.
If Europe were, if this EU were a functioning democracy, one would first have to ask the question of responsibility, and how all this could have come about.
It is simply not true that all the problems we now face would be a tsunami, something governed by natural law; rather, it is man-made.
And with all the regulatory frenzy that has characterized this Union, one has to ask, why was no action taken when banks began to cross national borders to a significant extent?
Why did one not act despite appropriate warnings when the derivatives had thus crept in?
Jürgen Mikuta, and Nobel laureate Josef Stiglitz, as you know, addressed all these points.
There was a silence, there was a taboo.
That's where one would have to start, because the key to a solution in the future lies solely in recognizing one's own mistakes.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, Mr Commissioner, my question relates to what you think of it if Iceland and Hungary were to introduce the Euro as a second currency.
Do you think that this could solve the problem of these two countries?

places at the center and that, above all, low-value economic goods are increased by a factor of 10, so that fictitious profits are not taxed, that the money remains in the companies and that thus wages can also be paid better.
Madam President, the comprehensive report illustrates how many human rights violations occur daily in the world.
This is particularly frightening 60 years after the declaration of the Human Rights Charter, and calls on us to further intensify our efforts.
Also for this reason, I would like to advocate for the current Sub-committee on Human Rights to be established as a main committee.
Today we awarded the Sakharov Prize to Hu Jia, who advocates for human rights and the environment in China.
However, as he is incarcerated, he could not personally take charge of him.
Shame on China. And tomorrow, a debate on human rights violations in Russia will take place here.
In our relations with these two countries, we must accord not only economic interests but above all human rights a higher priority.
We demand freedom for Hu Jia in China and freedom for Mikhail Khodorkovsky in Russia.

Mr. President, I would like to pick up on a remark that my Austrian colleague Paul Rübig made earlier.
We spend less than 1% of the European gross domestic product on Europe, and at the same time we demand that more and more tasks for more and more member states be handled by the European Union.
This calculation cannot work out. It is therefore also really incredible when we have been on an austerity course for many years, but the austerity course is not only implemented in a thrifty way
deploy our funds, but essentially to ensure that the finance ministers at the end of the year again pocket money and let it flow into their budgets at home.
We would have enough new tasks for which we should spend our money.
We also have tasks that for years we have not really adequately budgeted for.
I believe that the information funds of the European Union should be significantly increased if we really want to bind European citizens to Europe.
and there are many tasks in the field of student and apprentice exchange that Europe could undertake here.

This must not be unnecessarily jeopardized, merely because such professional groups do not exist elsewhere.
Therefore, integration and mutual opening, yes, but with moderation.
Thank you very much, Madam President, dear colleagues. Plant protection products have been and continue to be essential inputs in agriculture.
Farmers need a minimum selection of plant protection products, because it's about protecting the native cultural landscape, high-quality food, and protecting resources.
We conduct discussions about chemicals too often on a very irrational level.
But to get reasonable results, we must think and act rationally.
The effects of the original parliamentary position would have been fatal: a broad ban on plant protection products, leading to lower yields in agriculture, and ultimately rising food prices.
Therefore, I am pleased that a just acceptable compromise was found in the trilogue negotiations.
It is welcome that we have retained the three-zone regulation, albeit with significant exceptions for the member states.

A courageous step towards a consistent single market solution would look different at this point. Besides the acceptable compromises, we should not prejudge a definitive definition of endocrine-active substances, but rather actually give the EU Commission the four years to present a scientifically sound definition.
Otherwise, at this point, we overstretch the precautionary principle.
And lastly, I find it regrettable that we do not bring ourselves to initiate an impact assessment for the agreement now reached. Thank you.

2 minutes. Child and politics. Rarely do the interests of citizens and politicians diverge so widely.
Among citizens throughout Europe, the issue of the child is paramount.
For politicians, children's concerns rank as a low priority, as we can observe once again here.
I know no country that has its own Ministry for Children. Even in the Commission, there is no dedicated representative for the interests of our youngest citizens.
In parliament, the issue of children is scattered across all committees.
Therefore, I would like to particularly emphasize the Commission at this point, that it is today dealing with childcare facilities and the Barcelona target.
Thank you. In our parliamentary working group "Quality of Childhood," we have observed the following: Our children live in a very complex world.
The future of a person who is a child here, now, and today is completely uncertain.
A child of today can climb all the way to the top of the social scale or fall all the way down.
It can remain in its own cultural sphere or look for a new one.
It can take a similar life path to its parents, or do exactly the opposite.
One can marry someone of the opposite sex or of the same sex.
All of this was still completely different more than 50 years ago, when we launched the united Europe.
The diversity that distinguishes us Europeans – this diversity has become a determining factor in the lives of children.
The concept of diversity must therefore find its way into the pedagogy of early childhood educators and teachers.
This is currently still given far too little consideration.
The childcare facilities and schools in Europe are indeed breaking under the challenge of integration.
On integration issues, we must press the reset button, back to the start.
Let's approach this topic once again completely impartially, according to the motto "unity through diversity." And about that,

And beyond that, I ask all Euro parliamentarians who follow me not only to advocate for gender mainstreaming. Also fight for child mainstreaming, especially now in the time of the global financial crisis, because otherwise we will burden our children with immeasurable debts. Thank you.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. I want to make this very brief.

I would just like to send out two more messages at the end. Firstly, I expressly thank the Commissioner for not having fundamentally spoken against the concept of the minimum requirements of the safety net.

It is an instrument for problem-solving, to which we should give a chance.

And the second thing I want to say, and this is now directed at my British friends, namely, cross all the parties. My
My dear British friends, I do understand, Caroline. I do understand your problem.
I can well understand that the energy security of a country is, for a certain period of time, placed above compliance with an air pollutant limit value.
I have great understanding for that.
I am also the last one who would not grant Her Majesty additional time when it comes to building new coal-fired power plants.
We can talk about it.
The problem is, we have precisely not done that.
This conversation has never taken place during the last four months.
I would like to ask you – this is now the outlook in the second reading – to be open-minded again at this point and to say, let's talk about a compromise at that point and

for that, to abandon the resistance – which, in my view, is completely irrational and unjustifiable from a competition policy and environmental policy perspective – the fundamental opposition to the minimum standards.
I believe that this is the point where a compromise can be reached, which I hope will ultimately be adopted in the second reading.
By the way, I am not of the opinion that we shouldn't seek a compromise in a first reading.
This legislation is too complex for that.
I do not want to end this legislative term as rapporteur and leave the next parliament with legislation in which a legal text is discussed that is not consistent, is illogical, and contradictory.
Therefore, I ask for support for the compromises when we vote here in an hour. Thank you very much.
Yes, dear colleagues, I think the decisive question with all the economic stimulus programs that are now being put together Europe-wide is, of course, who ultimately receives the money?
Are banks still being issued further blank checks, even though rapid nationalization would ultimately be much cheaper for the taxpayer?
And should corporations and recipients of high incomes be further relieved, although they have been fattened for years throughout Europe by tax breaks.

The more money is squandered for such purposes, the more foreseeable it is that such programs will fail, and the more likely it is that the European economy will sink further and further down in a very dangerous downward spiral.

The years-long policy of privatization, deregulation, liberalization – precisely this policy has increasingly concentrated incomes among the top 10,000, and it was this policy that is responsible for the current crisis in which we find ourselves today.
And whoever believes that he can overcome this crisis by continuing precisely this policy with minor modifications, I think he simply understood nothing at all.
The exact opposite of that is needed.
Instead of buying toxic assets from the banks, tax funds should be used for the renovation of schools, of hospitals, and for the ecological transformation of the European economy.

As long as private companies receive public funds, the following must apply: No taxpayer money without employment guarantees, and above all, no taxpayer money without the acquisition of public ownership rights, so that the state and especially the general public can also participate in future returns.
The very best economic stimulus program that could currently exist would be a radical redistribution of income and wealth from top to bottom.
The low-wage sector in Europe must be pushed back instead of continuing to promote it.
We need higher minimum wages; we need better social benefits in Europe.

And we need taxes that ensure that the millionaires and the profiteers of the past financial market party now also bear the immense losses that have arisen, and not the large majority of citizens who, after all, never got anything from the entire financial boom.
I think that what is socially just is currently also the only economically and politically sensible option.
And it is the only way to break the devastating crisis dynamic.

And we agree with the fundamental concept of the Green Paper, according to which territorial cohesion is about the polycentric development of the EU.
Overall, as well as to ensure a sustainable development of the sovereign territories with various characteristics and peculiarities, and at the same time to preserve their diversity.

percent of the gross domestic product will be exceeded, must be created. So that these regions receive a clear status, and thus have more security in their development.
And in the report by colleague Krehe on obstacles in the utilization of structural funds, the most significant shortcomings are rightly listed, which applicants face when
in the utilization of the Structural Funds. For example, excessive administrative burden, too many complex regulations, slow and cumbersome centralized administration in the Member States.

I would like to briefly highlight and affirm two such measures.
First, currently, project managers must retain their project documents for a period of 10 years, in order to have them available for inspections that may be carried out by the Commission.
Especially for smaller projects, this is an excessively bureaucratic effort.
Correct, that this should now be reduced to a three-year period. And secondly, the assessment criteria of the EU Commission for innovative projects cause significant problems.
For innovative projects, the same assessment criteria must not apply as for other projects. Colleague,
It is simply necessary that something different occurs here.

Thank you very much, Madam President. When Europe provides funds and, above all, when the Member States provide a great deal of funds to help the automotive industry in its crisis-ridden situation, then it cannot just be about securing existing businesses and the status quo here and preventing bankruptcies, but it is about a series of other concerns.
The most important ones have been mentioned.
The aim is to secure this employment for people seeking work, as they need it, and to do so long-term.
For this, it is necessary that we promote new technologies, innovation, and above all, sustainability in the transport system more strongly than we have done so far.

Therefore, we should link all our measures with these objectives, so that in a few years' time we do not have to face the criticism that we should have thought of all this many years ago, then we would not be in the next, new crisis.
Thank you, Madam President. It would be nice if you would also grant me the additional half minute that my Green colleague, Ms. Breyer, just received.
The Commission had submitted a good proposal for updating the regulation on novel foods.

Unfortunately, some amendments attempt to supplement this proposal with provisions that are not compatible with the objectives of the regulation, or which even want to interfere with existing case law.
The obligation of food business operators to monitor novel foods, with a view to the health and welfare of animals, would simply overwhelm retailers.

And once again there is an attempt to undermine the existing GMO laws, namely the demand for special labeling of such novel foods that are derived from animals that were fed genetically modified feed.
We just heard it. I think,
Generally, everything that concerns GMO legislation should not be addressed in the Novel Food Regulation.
I say this, although I myself, for example, had submitted an application to specifically distinguish relevant new plant varieties from the definition of novel foods.
But it cannot be that interested circles within this House abuse the Novel Food Regulation for election campaign purposes.
Such an attempt is evidently also the case with amendments 62 and 90, for which the Greens have requested a roll-call vote.
Whom, esteemed colleagues, do you wish to pillory with that?
My faction has also always advocated for the consumer's right to know what is contained in food.
Why then should one not indicate a nano-origin in the declaration of ingredients?
And although Amendment 62 somewhat clumsily attempts to pre-empt my report on food labelling, I must tell you that I consider this labelling of Novel Food to be entirely compatible with my report.

I therefore recommend to my group the approval of the aforementioned motions, and will, in my report on the Food Labelling Regulation, include references to the part of the Novel Food Regulation that deals with labelling.

Policy coherence in the interest of development means that we do not take with one hand what we give with the other.
For example, it makes little sense to invest funding in the development of agriculture in developing countries if we simultaneously destroy local markets through direct or indirect export subsidies.
Likewise, we can campaign ever so hard for better healthcare, if we hinder the trade in generics or drive up drug prices to unaffordable levels through patent extensions.

And agrofuels might reduce CO2 emissions in the EU a little, but in developing countries lead to deforestation and land conversion, and thus to more climate change, which is precisely what we wanted to avoid. Not to mention the displacement of indigenous populations, loss of biodiversity, and arable land for food.
Currently, we – that is, the EU as such – are therefore not particularly coherent.
The EU has long recognized in theory that policy measures should not contradict each other.
In the Treaty of Lisbon, policy coherence in the interest of development is enshrined.
In Article 208, it states: "When implementing political measures that may affect developing countries, the Union takes into account the objectives of development cooperation."
We must be measured by this demand.
This year, we are reviewing the progress in achieving the Millennium Development Goals.
It is clear that without policy coherence, we will not succeed in fulfilling our promises.

We must become aware of this responsibility, all of us who pass and implement laws. Before we adopt a measure, there must be an impact assessment so that we can even recognize what European legislation can have negative effects on developing countries.
We need expertise in the Council, Commission, and Parliament that recognizes inconsistencies.
The Commission's work program on policy coherence is a good step.
And now it depends on how this plan is implemented.

Policy coherence in the interest of development means that we must give more consideration to the interests of local people in the fisheries reform and not subordinate them to the interests of European fishing corporations.
It means that we must dry up tax havens into which funds from developing countries flow.
It means that we cannot agree to ACTA if the suspicion remains that this agreement could disrupt access to medicines or technologies in developing countries.
So far, the Commission has not been able to dispel this suspicion.

Policy coherence also means that, when creating the External Action Service, development policy must remain a strong, independent policy area, and the competences of the Development Commissioner are expanded and not restricted.
And it also includes that we, for example, enable Fair Trade in the awarding of contracts in the municipalities, as my colleague Heike Rühle suggested.
In many cases, we Members of Parliament must keep a close eye on the Commission and the Council, so that they shape policy coherently.
In many cases, however, it is also up to us, the members of parliament, to be coherent in the interest of development.
We need to better network between the committees, and we need a standing female rapporteur.
The report, which the Development Committee unanimously adopted, makes many good suggestions and brings us a big step forward.
I would like to thank all shadow rapporteurs, the Ombudsman, and all the NGOs for their help and cooperation, and I hope that our joint report can also be adopted by the plenary tomorrow.

I would like to highlight two aspects.
One aspect is the environmental requirements for industrial plants,
It's about better implementing best available technology in Europe.

I believe that we have found an exception clause in the currently available compromise, which provides the EU Commission, Mr. Potočnik—and this is very clearly addressed to you—with the tool, in which

in the coming years, more strongly and better than before, to ensure that the approximately 52,000 industrial plants in Europe concerned by this ultimately use the best available technology better than they do today.
I have many arguments in this debate,
One thing I would like to address and also reflect here, because I cannot comprehend it at all.
I was often told that the use of the best available technology is too expensive, and that we would thereby endanger the competitiveness of European industry, even more so during a crisis.
I don't like to think about my past, but I come from the former GDR, from East Germany.
The economic and ecological collapse of the GDR was also attributable to the fact that for decades, investment in the latest state-of-the-art technology had been neglected.

And I believe that every industry, every industrial operation, is compelled by its very existence to invest in the latest state of technology, because otherwise it loses competitiveness, and thereby, of course, quite incidentally, also reduces pollutant emissions.
I think today we have the chance to enact a law that improves this initial situation.
The second part of the directive, and that's a bit like the clenched fist I mentioned, concerns large combustion plants.

We are once again talking – we already did this in the last directive – about renewed exceptions until the year 2020, and in some cases 2023, for large combustion plants that do not have to meet the environmental requirements, especially the air pollutant limit values.
Um, I am addressing the council here, but perhaps also a bit to all of us, that we need to consider very carefully how sensible that actually is, that we regularly

Ambitious environmental targets are welcome, which are also discussed and welcomed here, but whenever it comes to implementing them, to putting them into concrete legislation, then a tragedy begins in Europe.
Then the member states will suddenly realize what this costs, what bills they have to pay, and then, as a rule, those who have made advance payments will also be penalized.
There are a number of member states that are already implementing these requirements today and that will ultimately be left out in the rain.
And I'll say it again here, both in industrial plants and in large combustion plants, these are essentially coal-fired power plants; we are not talking about science fiction.

Best available techniques, that which is available on the market under competitive and cost aspects, can be installed in these plants. And against this background, we conclude
today a package of laws, which I believe is better compared to what is currently in force, and therefore I encourage us all to approve it.

Poverty, corruption, disregard for human and minority rights, authoritarian regimes, economic conditions, such as in Tajikistan and Kyrgyzstan, hundreds of thousands,

Thank you very much, Mr. President. Mr. Commissioner, I am very much in favor of green genetic engineering, but cloning, that is something else.
That cannot be compared with it at all.
Cloning is animal cruelty, and many colleagues have already said it here.
I also presented it before the summer break in the discussion we had. Only a fraction of the animal clones is actually viable.
And those who survive, a great many of them die very early and in great agony.
They suffer from malformation of extremities, of organs,
They have immune deficiencies, they are susceptible to illness.
This means pain and suffering, and precisely this agonizing death.
Can we really justify this?
We advocate so much for animal welfare, and then we want to support such a technology?
No, we do not want that, and that is why we decided in July that we do not want any cloned meat and no animal products from cloned animals.
And that is only consistent.

Yes, we know that products from cloned animals or their descendants are probably not harmful to health, and I personally assume that they would actually be suitable for human consumption. But, there is actually no shortage of meat from conventional production.
Why should we engage in this cloning?
That is completely unnecessary, to engage in this animal cruelty. And the population doesn't want it either.
the population does not want these products. We don't need them either. There is no argument for clone meat in the European Union.
And therefore we want a very clear regulation explicitly for cloned animals and yes, also for their offspring.
I want a ban on cloning and on cloned products in the European Union, and I also believe that we can enforce that.
Naturally, this must also apply to imports.
And I want to be sure that I'm not secretly given clone meat because it's not labeled, as has already happened here in
Scotland, and I do not believe that all meat can be traced back.
With beef, it may be possible.
But with pork, due to the quantity produced, it will not be possible to trace back whether it was actually once a clone in its ancestry.
Fundamentally, I think, yes, it is a fundamental question, it is an ethical question.
And other colleagues have pointed out that we must also address ethical questions, and we are also responsible for ethical questions.
And of course, there is the question of animal welfare, but there is also another question.

Experience shows, I am an animal producer. I have studied this. Experience shows that all reproductive techniques, as soon as they truly worked in animal production, were ultimately also applied to humans.
And so today we must ask ourselves: do we want and can we accept cloning technology in the not too distant future? Do we want and can we also accept that in humans?
So, I don't want that, and I can't do that either.
And even if one could clone a spare part for me, perhaps donate a vital organ in the future, a clone of myself, then I do not want that.
And I do not believe that this is an exaggerated horror vision.
I believe that this would be possible in the not too distant future if we were to allow cloning.
And therefore, we urgently need a ban not only on cloning, but also on cloned animals and their offspring throughout the entire EU.

Madam President, Mr. Commissioner Hahn, colleagues,
I have a problem with parts of this debate.
When we speak of mountain islands and sparsely populated regions, I get the impression from individual contributions that they are extraterritorial and directly subordinate to the European Union.
But that's not how it is.
Each of these territories belongs to a member state that is sovereign.
And the implementation of European resolutions and goals is, according to the principle of subsidiarity, still the responsibility of the member states.

If I consider the European level, we have always given special attention precisely to these areas: mountain regions, sparsely populated areas, islands. This has been the case in communications from the Commission right up to the Lisbon Treaty.
And here in parliament, many resolutions were passed in support of this region.

When I now hear many complaints here that there are still grievances in those areas, then the question arises for me: how have the member states dealt with the impulses that we sent from the European level—how did they deal with them?
And what have we—the regions themselves—proceeded with, and utilized the European funding opportunities.

A new strategy is being demanded here, so I already feel sorry for the officials of DG Regio, who are supposed to encompass the multitude of problems of islands, mountains, and sparsely populated areas into one strategy.

Simultaneously, we are witnessing a proliferation of strategies concerning the Danube, Baltic Sea, Black Sea, climate change, and many similar issues, so much so that we will soon need a strategy for these strategies themselves, in order to still maintain an overview.

Dear colleagues, jokes aside. I think what this is really about – and that has also been said today, for which I am very grateful to Commissioner Hahn for mentioning it – we must fight to ensure that there is a cohesion policy and a regional policy at the European level even after 2013.

undoubtedly, we have made progress in recent years regarding the equality of men and women. Nevertheless, there are still many women who are pushed into low-skilled employment.

In the deregulation of recent years, employment subject to social insurance contributions has frequently been transformed into atypical, precarious, and insecure employment relationships.
There, especially women,
The European employment development from 2000 to 2010 comprised 60% new, precarious, and atypical employment relationships, predominantly two thirds of which were women.

I note that the EPP unconditionally commits itself to the right to asylum and to the right to protection for those in need of protection.
The consideration of reality, however, unfortunately also leads us to the problem that the right to asylum is invoked for entirely different reasons or even deliberately misused.
The right to asylum and subsidiary protection is not an instrument for general immigration to the 27 member states.

Asylum procedures must be carried out professionally and carefully.

The authorities of the member states are given far too few opportunities to prevent abuse.
The possibility for carrying out accelerated procedures and border procedures shall be unduly restricted.

If an applicant massively violates their cooperation obligations, there are hardly any options for sanctions; quite the opposite. If they disappear illegally, the member state should no longer be able to conclude their procedure negatively, and when they reappear, a wider range of procedural options should be available to them.

Even in cases of obvious groundlessness, an accelerated procedure should be possible after the second subsequent application. An accelerated procedure should not be possible only after the second subsequent application.
This means significant cost increases.

I simply ask the Commission to consider the consequences of their proposals on practical reality, the financial implications, and the problems for the administrations of the Member States.
We want a high standard, but it must also be feasible, and above all, it must not confront particularly burdened member states with completely impossible tasks.

Yes, thank you very much. Colleagues, the demand for a financial transaction tax is not new; opponents of globalization have been demanding such a tax for many years already.
However, even five years ago, hardly anyone would have believed that it could be implementable in the foreseeable future.
But this week it is on the agenda in the European Parliament, together with a CO2 tax and Eurobonds, and I think that's good.
I can only welcome and support the financial instruments that Anni Polymata proposes in her report, and I think we will also vote on them accordingly.
They are urgently needed instruments,
The consolidation of public budgets has so far concentrated solely on cutting expenditures.
The cut in public spending, however, primarily affects employees, pensioners, and the poor. So, those who depend on the welfare state.
The revenue side of the public sector has so far been completely overlooked.
This is the second adjustment screw that can be turned to consolidate public budgets.
Above all, however, the main perpetrators of the public debt crisis can also be adequately involved in the repayment of public debt through the revenue side.
For the consolidation of public budgets, an increase in tax revenues is simply indispensable.

In fact, the high indebtedness of public budgets is primarily a consequence of the nationalization of private debts, namely the takeover of the debts of private banks and the financing of the consequences of the financial crisis by the public sector.
The responsibility for the debt crisis is therefore by no means borne by the states alone.
It is not only obvious, but also politically necessary to ask the financial sector to pay.
With the financial transaction tax, this sector would finally be involved in the repayment of these debts as the main cause of public debt.
This is an important political signal that emanates from this report if it is adopted as it has been presented.
But similarly, we consider Eurobonds to be a sensible and therefore also necessary instrument.
They contribute more to the reduction of debt than all sanctions or more or less well-intentioned advice.

The complaints of some surplus countries that their interest burdens would thereby increase through Eurobonds are, however, not acceptable, because at the same time these surplus countries also earn through their exports to the deficit countries.
This can then be criticized as a transfer union.
But whoever wants a social Europe, whoever wants the EU to also stick together in the future, must at least fundamentally agree to a transfer union.
Finally, I would also like to say to the Commission that I hope that it will now finally present the proposals for a financial transaction tax, which have been demanded for over a year. Thank you.

Dear Madam President, dear Commissioner Kruse, radio frequencies are a precious and limited resource that must be managed with care, foresight, and considering overall societal aspects.
Therefore, the Radio Spectrum Policy Program is accorded a very special significance.
Radio frequencies must be utilized efficiently, taking into account economic, social, and cultural aspects.

Many thanks, Madam President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues. It astonishes me that we always have to remind ourselves what is actually understood by structural and cohesion policy.

Cohesion and structural policy is one of the central policy areas of the European Union, and by definition, it is intended to serve the strengthening of economic and social cohesion within the community, and thereby especially promote growth and employment in the underdeveloped regions.
The necessity and success of cohesion and structural policy has proven itself particularly in the years of the crisis.
In many regions, it would still be much more difficult concerning economic development if these funds were not available.
The implementation of the funding structure takes place on the basis of operational programs – as has already been pointed out – in which the funding strategy of the respective region is described.
The European Union, member states, and regions collaborate here in partnership, thereby creating true European added value.
The success of the cohesion and structural policy is also based on the reliability of the programs and the funds made available and continuously available.

Therefore, I am very surprised when, time and again, by member states, but also by the Commission, as we now see, an attempt is made to utilize the structural fund resources like a piggy bank for all possible current policy approaches.

This does not do justice to the spirit and purpose of cohesion policy and, moreover, unsettles the actors in the regions who are already working towards achieving their objectives on the basis of long-term effective strategies and agreements.
Certainly, the fight against youth unemployment, the commitment to economic growth, is an important goal.
This will only be achieved if the partnership-based cooperation between the regions, the member states and the Commission is maintained, and the actors in the regions are and remain involved.
Only you have the knowledge about what is necessary to do in your regions.
Cohesion and structural policy is the unifying, partnership-based, solidarity-based bond of action between the individual policy levels in the European Union.
In it, Europe based on solidarity becomes visible locally in the regions, and it should not be carelessly abandoned due to political quick fixes.

The request made in 2009 for an amendment to the regulation on biometric passports, which called for a review to be carried out after three years and four studies to be presented, was supported by our parliamentary group.
And that's also why they had repeatedly raised significant concerns due to data protection reasons, and also because of the overestimation of the usefulness of biometric data in passports.
The belief in perfect identification, the overestimation of this method as well, and, what then follows from that, the underestimation of other links in the security chain, are truly problematic.
The handling of biometric data is still subject to this great misconception, namely the assumption that it is unalterable and most trustworthy.
And that is certainly not the case.
These data are also alterable throughout life.
In children, they simply look different to others than in old age.
And that is also why reliable and serious studies and conclusions from them are necessary.
Nevertheless, counterfeiting continues diligently.
We now have the inventory.
as incidents in Great Britain and other countries repeatedly prove, the chips are not at all secure against misuse.
They are readily cracked and can mean permanent damage for those affected.
Also, the standards within the EU are extraordinarily different.
The error rate is very, very high, as in France and the Netherlands.
Time and again, the question must be posed: what use is biometric data to us if documents are falsified and in circulation?
If one link in the chain is falsified and the source documents are inconsistent, biometric data would not be able to help us further.
The Chaos Computer Club in Germany only took a few minutes to demonstrate a forgery.

For this, the friends from the Chaos Computer Club needed the lid of a plastic bottle, superglue, a digital camera, and a little wood glue. In a few minutes, they had cracked the chip. So much for the supposed security.
We expect from the Commission the submission of the studies and, of course, the results and the consequences that arise from them. Thank you.

Numbers
sometimes say more than big words, and to this day, by the European Parliament – you also mentioned it – by the European Patent Office, pardon, almost 2,000 patents on plants and almost 1,200 patents on animals have been granted, which came into being both with and without the use of genetic engineering.
I may mention two prominent examples: the patenting of genetically modified and selectively bred barley. This applies to the entire chain from seed to plant.
Affected are therefore our farmers, just like our brewers, producers, as well as we consumers,
And this example, in my opinion, shows a devastating extent of patenting.
Second example, the so-called Long Life tomato, not to be confused with your Long Life oil that you have in the engines of your diesel engines.
With this trick of the applicant US corporation, Arcadia, this patent was developed bypassing valid EU rules.
What has been patented is not the tomato, but rather its genetic material, namely selected enzymes.

This shows what tricks are being employed here. And this, although, as you say, Mr. Commissioner, it is clearly stated in Article 4 of the 1998 Biopatent Directive that: are not patentable, plant varieties and animal breeds, A and B essentially biological processes for the breeding of plants or animals.
We believe that this definition must be specified; it must be interpreted more precisely.
Technical procedures used to support conventional breeding must not lead to overall patentability.
We say specifically: no patent on life.
And we need a clear signal here for more ethics and for more honesty in dealing with creation, once again against the patentability of life throughout Europe.
Therefore, Mr. Commissioner, we demand a clear statement from the Commission that patents on breeding material and genetic resources are and remain prohibited in Europe.
Secondly, we demand a clear position on Article 4 of the Biopatent Directive. No patent on life.
And thirdly, Commissioner, we demand on behalf of many farmers, many breeders in Europe, a clear codification of the breeder and farmer privilege in all legislation. Thank you.
Thank you, Madam Chair. I am making a statement on the discharge of Europol and Eurojust.

Only the high carry-forwards are noticeable in comparison to the overall actually low incurred costs.
Europol attributes this to the move to a new headquarters. That is, of course, understandable.
but Europol undertakes to take further measures in the future to avoid such high transfers.

regarding Eurojust. Here, the annual financial statement also reflects the financial situation in 2010 in all essential aspects.

Madam President,
distinguished Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen,
What must frighten us and put us in a panic is not the sheer number of unemployed young people in Europe alone,
but the development.
From 2008 to 2011, the number of unemployed young people rose by 26.5%. This was stated by the International Labour Organization.

It is this development that urgently requires measures. For we are not only disconnecting an entire generation from the labor market, we are disconnecting an entire generation from a future worth living.
If we give young people the feeling of absolute uselessness, we should not be surprised that young people turn away from this EU because they have no reasonable work and life prospects.

Mr. Commissioner, I urgently request this. Please ensure that decisive countermeasures are initiated. I already acknowledge some things, but please ensure that the long-standing intellectual lull does not now turn into operational frenzy.

When I read that the Commission is launching a program whereby young people, when they apply abroad, are now also to be financially supported, then we are organizing a traveling circus there that does not solve the problem.
And on the contrary, it becomes counterproductive. Technicians, doctors, engineers, and nursing staff are then poached by those states that are richer, that are not in this crisis.
What is needed now, in this crisis—which is the cause of this development—are offensive measures, specifically the dismantling of this savings frenzy, which is stifling the economy in Europe.
We need a targeted growth policy, we need a strengthening of domestic purchasing power, public investments in education, research, alternative energies.
That would be the way.
With the combination of European funding, it would then be possible to truly develop an integrated concept that creates a life perspective for young people.
Last remark, this youth unemployment is also incompatible with the nonsense of wanting to raise the retirement age for people to 70.
For that, we need a different policy that provides young people access to the labor market.
I strongly request this. Thank you.

is now in the hands of the Council to take action here, and I would like to use my speaking time to appeal to the Council to do everything possible to ensure that this tax is actually implemented and becomes a reality. Thank you.
Dear colleagues, I think that with all the detailed problems that can be discussed and that still need to be discussed in the further legislative process, we should agree on one thing:

to be loaded.

and that is why I advocate for this proposal today with a large majority
The reform of the General System of Preferences was quite necessary.
It is now good that there are longer terms and that there is more security for exporters.
However, the criteria are too imprecise, as the Development Committee has already stated.
Unfortunately, the Commission refers only to World Bank classification.
In this regard, the parliament has in the past and repeatedly very critically engaged with the gross domestic product as the sole criterion.
The definition of countries with upper-middle income can refer to countries with $4,000 or up to $12,000 Gross Domestic Product.
This is therefore a huge and far too wide range. And it in no way does justice to the realities on the ground.
We certainly do not find it concerning that countries like Saudi Arabia and Kuwait find this out.
The reform in that regard was therefore good.
But these countries are high-income countries anyway.
Russia and China, however, are countries with an upper-middle income, about whose falling out of the APS we certainly also do not have to worry.
But this category of countries also includes much poorer countries, such as Namibia and Ecuador.
Up to now, the Commission had at least also considered the vulnerability criterion. So, countries that export little or export only very few goods.
This at least prevents countries that are dependent on the export of fewer products and are very vulnerable from being classified too highly.
Why then did the Commission strike out this criterion?
Countries such as the Dominican Republic, Fiji Islands, Gabon, and Botswana will thus fall out, although their economy falls under the criterion of vulnerability.
Why did the Commission not continue the old criteria, which were already somewhat insufficient anyway?
The suspicion arises that the Commission wants to force poorer countries into free trade agreements that are less favorable for them.

The preference system is, however, an instrument for the promotion of development and cannot be replaced by mere trade instruments, where then the development path of a country or a region no longer plays any role at all.
Fortunately, however, the colleagues in the plenary will still have the opportunity to approve our amendment, which includes vulnerability as a criterion.
The new regulation, however, also leads to trade regions being torn apart.
Neighboring countries in customs unions will soon have to follow different rules when exporting to the EU.
That has nothing to do with our goal of regional integration.
And the Commission should increasingly consider development regions instead of individual countries.
Here too, we Greens have introduced an amendment, which will hopefully find much approval.
And it is also problematic when countries that still benefited from the APS yesterday are suddenly supposed to drop out of the program overnight.
Transitional periods must exist, and we see in our own countries that just a few months are not enough for economic development.

Ultimately, however, we as parliamentarians must also consider whether, with all legislative projects, we only focus on speed and then work with flimsy compromises, or whether we would prefer to involve the entire plenum.

I believe that for legislation, the entire Parliament is important, for democracy as well, and it's not time and not speed that is important, but rather it needs time to implement democracy and to properly pass laws. Thank you very much.

Please. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, colleagues. In June 2010, the heads of government of the EU finally recognized that the European Community needs a better coordinated financial and economic policy.
At the summit, they virtually invented the European Semester.
It is indeed entirely correct that the members of an economic and monetary union must not solely focus on how their policies affect businesses, employees, and consumers in their own country.
A European perspective is necessary.

A single market without coordination in economic, wage, and social policy presents the member states with a great temptation, namely to create short-term growth and jobs at the expense of their neighbors.
Such imbalances can bring the currency union to collapse, economically as well as politically.
And the EU is currently confronted with precisely such a situation.

The German economy has undermined its neighbors for years, with no impetus from investments and wage growth. Instead, Berlin has almost driven the Eurozone to ruin with a decade of aggressive export policy.

Madam President,

Many thousands of people could still be alive today, or would not have permanent disabilities, if professional medical help had been provided quickly enough after their accident, that is, within the first so-called golden hour.
We would be a whole lot closer to Vision Zero.
Now, the Ecall system that we are talking about today cannot prevent accidents. It also does not exempt one from the first aid obligation.
It serves solely to automatically inform an emergency control center in the event of a serious accident.
This must also, in order to avoid misuse and overloading due to minor accidents, establish verbal contact with the accident vehicle.
Once the emergency call is confirmed or no one responds further, emergency doctors, equipped with little but life-critical information, are dispatched to the scene of the accident.
The responsibility for the functioning of this rescue chain lies subsidiarily with the Member States.
As long as the sensors installed in the vehicle do not detect an accident, the eCall system remains dormant. It therefore sends no signals or data whatsoever and cannot be located.
It is therefore, even from a data protection perspective, far superior to a mobile phone.
Only with the automatic, sensor-controlled awakening or the conscious manual triggering does the system become active.
It then sends a pre-determined minimal data set of information to an emergency control center.
Europe-wide uniform standards and specifications will be guaranteed, that this data, upon arrival, is recognized as an emergency call, evaluated, and handled.
Therefore, the member states must also accordingly upgrade and expand their emergency service chains.

We want this eCall emergency call system to be based on the EU-wide emergency number 112, to be free of charge for the user, to function everywhere in the EU, and to be compatible with the emergency call systems of neighboring third countries.
It should be based on an interoperable technology and a neutral, open system architecture.
Through these, other intelligent public transport services such as real-time traffic information or the availability of parking spaces can also be offered later.

In recognition of the outstanding achievements of the automotive industry in the provision of private emergency call systems, these should retain their right to exist in parallel with the public 112 eCall.
Users can also decide for such a system on the basis of detailed information.
This may also be accompanied by costs, where applicable.

Thank you very much, Madam President. Through the new directive, a basis is created for dealing with books, newspapers, works of art, films, pieces of music, TV productions, which are indeed protected by copyright, but for which the rights holder cannot be identified or found.
Therefore, books, films, photos, or other intellectual creations for which no rights holder can be found anymore should also be made available to the public.
And so a cultural treasure is recovered.
For us in the Culture Committee, it was particularly important, in this legislative initiative, to highlight the special significance of the diversity of the European Union's cultural heritage.

In the archives of our museums, libraries, publishing houses, and broadcasting institutions, there are countless unused works, and merely because the rights holder is unknown, they have not been able to be made available so far.
This changes with the guideline.
It enables future generations to use this cultural asset if it is ensured that the publication is also preceded by a careful search.
This is a very important point, so that it is not carelessly stated, "we cannot find the rights holder."
For a balance had to be found between the actually existing rights of the creators of works and the public's need to also be able to use such works where the rights situation cannot be clarified.
A particular focus for us is also on the fact that we had to ensure that both known and unknown rights holders continue to be fully protected.
And where the copyright holders cannot be found, the draft enables the use of the digitization of orphan works.
Projects like Europeana and other large European digitization projects, but also our museums, archives, and educational institutions, as well as public broadcasting, will be included.
In the future, however, it must also be about opening the orphaned works in the archives of private publishers to the public.
This draft directive and the compromise that has now been reached is, in my view, truly a compromise and a first step in the right direction.
And I believe that the cultural treasure that belongs to it holds enormous potential,
And we should also continue to work on that during the revision of the guideline in two years' time. Thank you very much.

Mr. President, colleagues, five years ago, the EU's Central Asia strategy was launched.
If one asks today about the outcome of this strategy, the assessment turns out to be mixed.

We see how the member states individually endeavor to establish good economic relations, particularly with the resource-rich countries of this region, while on the other hand, the efforts are not advancing.
to accompany states on their transformation process towards modern democratic societies, or, as recent events in Kazakhstan show, the development is even characterized by setbacks.

Nevertheless, precisely for this reason, the European Union and its member states must jointly assert their influence more strongly in this region, also, and especially, towards Kazakhstan, which has meanwhile become a main supplier for many EU states.

An isolation of Kazakhstan by the EU would only lead to competitors for influence in this geopolitically important country, for whom the insistence on upholding civil and human rights is not so important, being able to assert their influence even more strongly than before.
The combination of economic interests with the commitment to uphold civil rights is the core of the Central Asia strategy.
That should also be the basis for further negotiations for a partnership and cooperation agreement with Kazakhstan.
The present report offers good guidance on this. Thank you very much.
My ladies and gentlemen, of the absent Council. Perhaps you are following us on the Parliament's video stream, as you obviously have neither the capacity nor the desire to speak live with us.
I consider the absence of the Council here today as a gross disregard for Parliament.
Instead of evading this debate, they should rather reveal their secret as to why they refuse any improvement to the protection of personal data in the area of police and justice via a directive.

Police and the judiciary have been for years – we know that – an Eldorado of violations in the data protection sector. Not only with SWIFT, but for example, on a daily basis, data of citizens who, for example, demonstrate against Nazis, are unlawfully collected and stored.
We need the same protection standards for the public and private sectors, and for the police and judiciary. That's what it's about, to be able to guarantee civil rights.
If you, the Council, demand from Parliament to constructively participate in data protection-relevant regulations such as EU PNR or Europol, then the reverse must surely also apply to the Council.
Therefore, I demand that you finally put an end to the blockade policy in the data protection sector.
